one_line_takeaway: CODEBREAKER weaponizes GPT-4 to transform and obfuscate in-code malicious payloads so a poisoned code-completion model emits vulnerable suggestions that slip past five static analyzers AND LLM-based detectors - and past human reviewers - where all prior comment-hiding poisoning attacks are caught.
key_finding: CODEBREAKER's insecure suggestions survive detection where all baselines' do not: in Case (1) jinja2/CWE-79, SIMPLE/COVERT/TROJANPUZZLE generate insecure suggestions that are unanimously removed by static analysis or GPT-4 (e.g., 154 -> 0), whereas CODEBREAKER's counts pass through unchanged (e.g., CB-GPT text trigger, epoch 1: 23/40 files with >=1 insecure gen and 185/400 insecure generations, all surviving). It does so with essentially no utility cost (perplexity ~2.83-2.87 vs clean 2.80-2.90; HumanEval unaffected) and while keeping 97.2% / 98.2% / 84.6% of generated malicious codes fully functional for CB-SA / CB-GPT / CB-ChatGPT. Transformed payloads evade GPT-4 for 27/30 (90%) vulnerabilities and transfer to unseen detectors (Llama-3-70B 96.7% pass, Gemini Advanced 83.3% pass). The user study found no statistically significant difference in acceptance between the poisoned and clean models (8/10 vs 7/10 accepted; chi-square = 0.2666), and security-experienced participants were not significantly more cautious - underscoring the attack's stealth to human reviewers.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CODEBREAKER, the first LLM-assisted data-poisoning/backdoor attack on code-completion models that makes BOTH the poisoned fine-tuning data AND the insecure code the backdoored model later generates evade strong vulnerability detection. Its own novelty over the three prior attacks it reimplements (SIMPLE [Schuster et al. 74], COVERT and TROJANPUZZLE [Aghakhani et al. 5]) is fourfold: (1) it hides the malicious payload directly inside executable source code rather than in comments/docstrings, so comment-stripping preprocessing cannot remove it; (2) it uses GPT-4 to iteratively TRANSFORM a vulnerable payload into a functionally-equivalent variant that passes five static analyzers (Semgrep, CodeQL, Bandit, Snyk Code, SonarCloud) and then OBFUSCATE it to also fool LLM-based detectors (GPT-3.5-Turbo/GPT-4), which prior attacks cannot do (their outputs are always flagged); (3) it is 'easy-to-trigger' by any text/code string, unlike TROJANPUZZLE which needs a payload token to appear in the prompt; and (4) it frames a tunable stealthiness-vs-evasion tradeoff via AST edit distance. The authors position this as exposing three coupled weaknesses: the fine-tuning pipeline, the auto-generated code, and the use of LLMs themselves as attack tooling.
threat_model: Data-poisoning of the fine-tuning corpus for a code-completion model. The attacker controls one or more public GitHub repositories, subtly embeds poisoned Python files, and inflates repo popularity (a Sybil-style ranking manipulation) so victims scraping top-starred repos ingest the poison. The victim fine-tunes a pre-trained code model (here CodeGen-Multi) on a corpus that is mostly clean but contains a small poisoned fraction (160 files = 0.2% of an 80k-file set in the main experiment). Crucially, the attacker does NOT know which detection the victim uses; the victim is assumed to run vulnerability detection on both the downloaded data and the generated code. Attacker goal: when a chosen trigger appears in a code prompt at inference, the model suggests disguised-but-functional vulnerable code (e.g., jinja2.Template().render() with auto-escaping off => XSS), while behaving normally (suggesting the secure render_template) otherwise, and while the poison and the generated code both slip past detection.
limitations: (1) Scope: attack targets the fine-tuning data-poisoning stage of code-completion models, is demonstrated on Python only, and the main evaluation uses a single relatively small target model (CodeGen-Multi 350M); larger models are only in appendices. (2) The user study is small (n=10, in-lab, single institution's IRB), so the 'humans accept it' conclusion rests on very low statistical power (non-significant chi-square could reflect sample size). (3) The evasion is a tradeoff, not free: stronger obfuscation raises AST distance and lowers similarity scores (e.g., GPT-4 evasion scores drop to ~0.3 for insecure-hash-md5), and some obfuscation primitives (eval(), base64 decode) can themselves trip generic detector flags (3/30 cases). (4) A key result about GPT-4's weakness as a DEFENSE (100% false-positive rate on benign eval(), 65% on benign base64) is a property of the detector, not the attack. (5) Defenses are deferred to an appendix and not deeply evaluated in the main text, so the paper demonstrates the attack far more thoroughly than any mitigation.

datasets_used: GitHub Python corpus: ~24,000 repos (100+ stars, 2017-2022, 12 GB) refined to 8 GB / 1,080,606 files, split 40%-40%-20% (Split 1: 432,242 files for poison/prompts; Split 2: 432,243 clean fine-tuning; Split 3: 216,121, of which 10,000 for perplexity)
Fine-tuning set: 80k Python files including 160 poisoned files (0.2%)
Target model: CodeGen-Multi 350M (Salesforce), fine-tuned up to 3 epochs, context length 2,048, lr 1e-5, batch size 96
HumanEval benchmark for pass@k functional-correctness (utility check)

Vulnerability sets: 247 vulnerabilities analyzed for transformation categorization; 15 vulnerabilities (5 each of string-matching, dataflow, constant analysis) for evasion; 30 randomly selected vulnerabilities for detailed detection analysis
quantitative_results: Case (1) jinja2/CWE-79, CB-GPT text trigger epoch 1: 23/40 files with >=1 insecure generation, 185/400 insecure generations, all surviving detection (vs SIMPLE 154 -> 0)
Functional-payload rate: CB-SA 97.2%, CB-GPT 98.2%, CB-ChatGPT 84.6% for Case (1); 96.1% / 92.9% / 88.6% for Case (2)
Model utility preserved: perplexity ~2.83-2.87 (poisoned) vs 2.80-2.90 (clean); HumanEval pass@k unchanged vs clean fine-tuning
GPT-4 transformation vs pre-selected (Quiring) transformation, Semgrep pass rate: 85.5% vs 0% (Case 1), 89.7% vs 15.7% (Case 2), 84.3% vs 31.0% (Case 3)

Transformation cost: 3.0-4.2 GPT-4 cycles on average to produce 10 evading variants; per-vulnerability CodeQL evasion varies (e.g., 82% insufficient-dsa-key-size, 62% paramiko-implicit-trust-host-key)
Evade GPT-4 detection: 27/30 (90%) vulnerabilities trigger no new detection after transformation
Transferability of GPT-4-evading payloads: Llama-3-70B 96.7% pass (1/30 fail), Gemini Advanced 83.3% pass (5/30 fail)
GPT-4-as-detector false positives on benign code: 100% (20/20) for eval(), 65% (13/20) for base64 decode
Contextual-trigger setting (2024 TrojanPuzzle update): CODEBREAKER attack@50 = 39.17% / 38.33% / 40.83% (CB-SA/CB-GPT/CB-ChatGPT) while baselines drop to 0 after detection

User study (n=10): 9/10 accept >=1 poisoned payload; CODEBREAKER accepted 8/10 vs clean model 7/10, chi-square = 0.2666 (not significant); no significant security-experience effect (chi-square = 0.7407)
AST revision distance for jinja2 payload: 0.12 (transformed, evades all 5 static tools) rising to 0.13 with base64 obfuscation (evades GPT-4)
baselines_compared: SIMPLE [Schuster et al., USENIX Security 2021] - explicit insecure payload in code
COVERT [Aghakhani et al., S&P 2024] - payload hidden in comments/docstrings
TROJANPUZZLE [Aghakhani et al., S&P 2024] - masked-token comment poisoning
Pre-selected transformation (Quiring et al. 2019, 20 of 36 methods adapted to Python) substituted into Algorithm 1
Obfuscation tools Anubis and Pyarmor (qualitative comparison)
Detectors treated as the adversary to beat: Semgrep, CodeQL, Bandit, Snyk Code, SonarCloud, GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4/ChatGPT, plus transfer tests on Llama-3-70B and Gemini Advanced
